title: The practice of celebrating small victories without minimizing them
author: Kalesh

 I used to hate the phrase "celebrate small victories." Honestly, it made my skin crawl. Every time a therapist or...
htmlContent:
Why small victories feel so dangerous

I used to hate the phrase "celebrate small victories." Honestly, it made my skin crawl. Every time a therapist or a well-meaning friend told me to acknowledge my tiny wins, I felt this hot flash of shame. Like they didn't get it. Like they thought my trauma was something I could just "positive think" my way out of.

Here's the thing. When you've lived through complex trauma, small victories don't feel small. They feel like landmines. You take a shower when you've been in bed for three days. You send one email instead of spiraling for hours. You eat something that isn't sugar or caffeine. And your brain immediately goes: "So what? That's what normal people do. Why are you proud of basic functioning?"

I remember the first time I told my therapist I'd made my bed for three consecutive days. I braced for her to roll her eyes. Instead she said: "That's a big deal. Your nervous system just learned it can be consistent without something terrible happening."

That landed differently. She wasn't minimizing the rest of my pain. She was recognizing what it took for my body to trust enough to do something that small.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma fragments our sense of time and safety. When you've been through prolonged abuse or neglect, your nervous system doesn't know how to hold a small win without waiting for the other shoe to drop. You've learned that any moment of peace is just the calm before the storm. So celebrating feels dangerous. It feels like you're tempting fate.

But here's what I've found. The problem isn't celebrating small victories. The problem is that we've been taught to celebrate them in ways that erase the rest of our experience. We've been told to "just focus on the positive" or "look on the bright side." That's toxic positivity dressed up as self-help. That's not healing. That's gaslighting yourself.

The difference between minimizing and acknowledging

Let me be clear about something. Minimizing your pain is not the same as acknowledging your progress. They feel similar in the body, especially when you're used to being dismissed. But they're different.

Minimizing sounds like: "It's not that bad. Other people have it worse. Just be grateful for what you have." That's the voice of the inner critic, the one Pete Walker writes about in his work on C-PTSD. That voice wants you to stay small, to never claim anything good because claiming good means you might lose it.

I want to talk about something that doesn't get said enough in recovery spaces. Healing can feel like loss. When you start to let go of your survival strategies - the hypervigilance, the people-pleasing, the emotional walls - it can feel like you're losing yourself. Because those strategies were you, for a very long time. They were how you knew yourself.

Gabor Mate writes about this beautifully. The true self that got buried under survival adaptations - it's still there. But meeting it can be disorienting. Who am I without the anxiety? Who am I without the need to control everything? Who am I when I'm not performing for someone else's approval? These are terrifying questions. And they don't have quick answers.

But here's what I've found. On the other side of that disorientation is something real. Something that doesn't need to perform or protect or prove. Something that can just be. It's quiet. It's simple. And it's been waiting for you this whole time. You don't have to find it all at once. You just have to be willing to keep looking. Even when it's scary. Especially when it's scary.

Acknowledging sounds like: "I did a hard thing today. It was hard because of what I've been through. And I'm still struggling with other things. But this one moment, this one action, that was real."

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Walker breaks down exactly how the inner critic operates, and why acknowledging small wins without falling into toxic positivity is a skill we have to rebuild from scratch.

I've had to learn to check in with my body when I try to celebrate something. Not in a woo-woo way. Just a simple scan. Does my chest tighten? Do I feel the urge to immediately list everything I failed at today? Do I want to minimize what just happened? If yes, then I know the inner critic is hijacking the moment. And I can pause.

I can say to myself: "I see you, critic. You're trying to protect me from disappointment. But right now, I'm going to let myself have this for five seconds. Five seconds of 'good job.' That's it. Then you can have your say."

Wild, right? Giving the critic a time limit. It works because you're not fighting the critic. You're just setting a boundary.

Your nervous system needs proof of safety

Here's something Stephen Porges and Deb Dana have shown us through Polyvagal Theory. Your nervous system learns safety through small, repeated experiences. Not through big dramatic breakthroughs. Not through one perfect day. Through tiny moments where your body registers: "Oh, nothing bad happened. I'm still okay."

When you celebrate a small victory, you're not just being nice to yourself. You're teaching your nervous system a new pattern. You're saying: "This good thing happened. And I'm acknowledging it. And the world didn't end. And I'm not being punished for feeling good."

That's huge. That's rewiring decades of conditioning.

But here's the catch. If you force yourself to celebrate in a way that feels fake or forced, your nervous system will reject it. It knows when you're lying to yourself. It knows when you're performing positivity instead of actually feeling it.

So the practice has to be honest. It has to include the complexity.

For me, that looks like: "I got out of bed and brushed my teeth. That's good. I'm also still sad and angry about what happened yesterday. Both things are true. I don't have to pick one."

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She talks about the RAIN practice, which helps you hold both the pain and the progress without collapsing into either one.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did you feel a small release? Or did you tense up? Both are fine. Both are information.

The trap of comparison and the myth of "enough"

One of the biggest reasons we can't celebrate small victories is comparison. We look at other people who seem to have their lives together and think: "I'm so far behind. My 'victory' is their Tuesday morning."

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

I do this constantly. I see someone post about finishing a book or running a marathon or building a business, and I look at my day where I managed to do the dishes and not dissociate. And I feel like a failure.

But here's what I'm learning. Comparison is a trauma response. It's your brain trying to find safety by measuring yourself against others. It's the same part of you that learned to scan for threats in your childhood home. You're not broken for doing it. You're human.

Think about it. When you compare your small win to someone else's big win, you're asking your nervous system to feel safe by being "better than" someone. That's a fragile foundation. It will always collapse. Because there will always be someone ahead of you. And there will always be someone behind you.

The only comparison that matters is: compared to last week, last month, last year, did I move in a direction that feels more alive? Not perfect. Just more alive.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. He talks about how every part of you, even the critic that compares and minimizes, is trying to protect you. When you understand that, you can stop fighting yourself long enough to actually celebrate.

Stay with me here. I know this is uncomfortable. Sitting with the smallness of our wins can feel like admitting we're not enough. But that's the lie. The smallness is the point. The smallness is where healing actually happens.

A practical way to practice that doesn't feel fake

I'm not going to give you a five-step system or a journaling prompt that promises to change your life in seven days. That's not how complex trauma works. But I can tell you what I've found helpful.

I keep a running list on my phone. Not a gratitude list. A "things I did that were hard even though they look small" list. Some entries: "Took a shower when I didn't want to." "Responded to a text instead of ghosting." "Didn't buy something I didn't need to feel better." "Let myself cry for five minutes without judging it."

I don't look at this list every day. Sometimes I forget about it for weeks. But when I'm in a spiral, when I feel like I haven't done anything right, I scroll through it. And I see evidence. Real evidence. Not toxic positivity. Just facts.

Here's the rule I follow: I don't add anything to the list that I don't genuinely feel proud of. Even if it's a tiny pride. Even if it's mixed with shame or doubt. If there's even a sliver of authentic acknowledgment, it counts. If it feels like a lie, I leave it out.

This matters because your nervous system can tell the difference between genuine recognition and forced positivity. Forced positivity creates more shame. Genuine recognition, even if it's small, builds safety over time.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She gives concrete exercises for regulating your nervous system, which is exactly what you need when you're trying to hold a small win without spiraling.

I'll be honest. Some days I can't celebrate anything. Some days the list stays empty and I just survive. That's okay. That's not failure. That's reality. The practice isn't about forcing celebration every single day. It's about building the capacity to celebrate when it's genuine, and to rest when it's not.

You don't have to be grateful for your trauma. You don't have to pretend everything is fine. You just have to let yourself have the small moments of okayness without immediately taking them away.

That's it. That's the whole practice. It's not glamorous. It's not a quick fix. But it's real. And real is what heals.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide to understanding and recovering from complex trauma
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's introduction to Internal Family Systems therapy
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - Tara Brach's approach to embracing ourselves with compassion
  • Anchored (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical polyvagal guide to nervous system regulation

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
